steps.title: Identify the sentence boundary
steps.content: The blank falls between two complete independent clauses: "What increases is the rate at which the water turns to [blank]" and "a pressure cooker pot, though, an airtight seal traps the vapor in the pot, creating pressure that allows the temperature of the water to increase past its boiling point." Two independent clauses must be separated by a sentence-ending punctuation mark, not a conjunction or a comma alone.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — vapor. With: A period ends the first sentence cleanly; "With a pressure cooker pot, though" begins the second sentence correctly, with "though" as a parenthetical contrast word. ✓
  • Choice B — vapor with: No punctuation between the two clauses produces a run-on sentence — violates the rule that independent clauses must be separated by a period, semicolon, or conjunction with a comma. ✗
  • Choice C — vapor, with: A comma alone between two independent clauses is a comma splice — a comma cannot join main clauses without a coordinating conjunction. ✗
  • Choice D — vapor and with: "And with" is not a standard coordinating conjunction phrase; when "and" joins two main clauses it requires a comma before it, and "and with" creates an ungrammatical construction. ✗
A is correct.
